question: What is a signal in the context of digital signal processing?

choices.A: A physical wave that travels through air
choices.B: Information sent through a medium, represented as a sequence of numbers

question: Give the example of signals for air pressure/movement

choices.B: Image

question: Give the example of signals for electromagnetic radiation

choices.B: Image

question: Give the example of signals for acceleration, rotation

choices.B: Image

question: Give the example of signals for social or financial data

choices.B: Image

question: Which of the following is an example of a 1D signal?

choices.B: A color photograph

question: When signal is assumed as a mathematical model, what is its definition?

choices.A: Signals are random variations in voltage over time
choices.B: Signals are digital sequences stored in computer memory
choices.C: Signals are physical waves that travel through air only
choices.D: Signals are a relevant quantity y (air pressure, pixel value), as a function of time (y = f(t)) or space (y = f(x,y))

question: In the mathematical model of a signal, what does the notation y = f(t) represent?

choices.A: y is the frequency and t is the time period
choices.B: y is the amplitude and t is the sampling rate
choices.C: y is a relevant quantity (like air pressure) that depends on time t
choices.D: y is the energy and t is the temperature

question: In the mathematical model of a 2D image signal, what does y = f(x, y) represent?

choices.A: The brightness value at spatial coordinates (x, y)
choices.B: The x and y coordinates of the camera position
choices.C: The red and blue color channels
choices.D: The horizontal and vertical resolution in pixels per inch

question: What is the key difference between a 1D audio signal and a 2D image signal in mathematical terms?

choices.A: Audio uses complex numbers while images use real numbers
choices.B: Audio is a function of one variable (time), while an image is a function of two variables (spatial coordinates)
choices.C: Audio signals are always periodic while images are not
choices.D: Audio signals have higher amplitude than images

question: In the function y = f(t) for an audio signal, what physical quantity does 'y' typically represent?

choices.B: The air pressure variation at a point in space over time
choices.D: The wavelength of the sound wave

question: In the function I(x, y) for a grayscale image, what does I represent?

choices.A: The intensity or brightness at pixel position (x, y)
choices.B: The distance from the camera to the object at (x, y)
choices.D: The x and y coordinates of the image center

question: What makes a video signal three-dimensional in mathematical terms?

choices.A: It has red, green, and blue color channels
choices.B: It is a function of two spatial dimensions and time: I(x, y, t)
choices.C: It has height, width, and depth information
choices.D: It can be played in 3D glasses

question: What does signal processing combine?

choices.B: Only computer science

question: In the context of signals, what is noise?

choices.B: Any unwanted component that interferes with the signal

question: Which of the following is a common application of signal processing in finance?

choices.B: Predicting stock prices using moving averages

question: What is the purpose of a moving average filter in finance?

choices.A: To increase the frequency of data
choices.B: To remove short-term fluctuations and highlight long-term trends
choices.C: To add noise to the data

question: In games, how is DSP used for reverb?

choices.A: To increase the pitch of sounds
choices.B: To simulate room acoustics based on game environment

question: What do signals often contain...

choices.B: Image

question: What does an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) do?

choices.B: Converts analog signals to digital numbers

question: Which of the following is NOT a typical function of DSP?

choices.B: Noise reduction

question: What is a physical wave?

choices.B: A process where movement travels through a medium and the medium returns to its previous state

question: In a longitudinal wave, the movement of particles is:

choices.A: Perpendicular to the direction of travel
choices.B: Parallel to the direction of travel

question: In a transversal wave, the movement of particles is:

choices.A: Perpendicular to the direction of travel
choices.B: Parallel to the direction of travel

question: Give an example of a transversal wave

choices.B: Air

question: What happens to the particles in a medium when a wave passes?

choices.B: They oscillate around a fixed point
choices.D: They move in a straight line

question: What does frequency measure in a wave?

choices.B: The speed of oscillation (how many cycles per second)

question: What is amplitude?

choices.B: The length of oscillations

question: What does stronger oscillation mean in terms of sound?

choices.B: Quieter sound

question: What does amplitude represent in a sound wave?

choices.B: The loudness

question: What does amplitude represent in a light wave?

choices.B: The brightness

question: What does amplitude represent in a movement?

choices.B: The length of movement

question: What does amplitude represent in a change?

choices.B: The length of change

question: What does period (p) mean?

choices.A: It is the duration of a periodic signal's cycle
choices.B: It is the length of cycle
choices.D: The number of cycles per time (f = 1/p)

question: What does frequency (f) mean?

choices.A: It is the duration of a periodic signal's cycle
choices.B: It is the length of cycle
choices.D: The number of cycles per time (f = 1/p)

question: If a signal has a higher frequency, what happens to its period?

choices.B: It decreases

question: What is the unit of frequency?

choices.B: Hertz

question: Hertz is a unit for frequency, how is defined?

choices.B: Oscillation cycles per hour

question: How is the frequency of osccilation related to its period?

choices.B: frequency = 1 * period

question: If a signal has a frequency of 10 Hz, what is its period?

choices.B: 0.1 seconds

question: How is the energy of a discrete-time signal defined?

choices.B: The sum of squares of the signal values - sum t (f(t)^2)

question: What is signal power?

choices.B: The square of amplitude

question: If you double the amplitude of a signal, what happens to its energy?

choices.B: It quadruples

question: What does decibel (dB) measure?

choices.B: A logarithmic ratio of two quantities

question: What does a/b = x dB mean?

choices.B: It means x = 1 log10(a/b)

question: What does adding 10 dB corrspond to?

choices.B: It means multiplying by factor 30

question: If signal A has 10 times the energy of signal B, how many dB greater is A?

choices.B: 20 dB

question: Approximately how many dB correspond to a factor of 2 in energy?

choices.B: 3 dB

question: How much of the original energy of a signal do we get when we double its amplitude?
Which factor corresponds to 30dB?
How many Decibels correspond to a factor of 1,000,000
If you added 20dB, what would that correspond to?
What is the frequency range of human hearing?

choices.B: 0 Hz to 10 kHz

question: What are basic properties of osccilating signals (waves)

choices.B: Frquency, Amplitude, Period

question: What is an octave in music?

choices.B: A halving of frequency

question: How is amplitude measured?

choices.A: Often measures at crest and through peaks
choices.B: Image

question: What is the reference frequency for middle A in music?

choices.B: 440 Hz

question: In the human ear, which part converts mechanical vibrations to nerve signals?

choices.B: Cochlea

question: What is masking in audio perception?

choices.A: The ability to hear all frequencies equally
choices.B: A strong sound making nearby weaker sounds inaudible
choices.C: The reflection of sound off walls
choices.D: The process of converting analog to digital

question: How is masking used in audio compression like MP3?

choices.B: To remove frequencies that are masked, reducing data

question: What does a sine wave sound like in audio?

choices.A: A complex sound with many harmonics
choices.B: A pure tone with no overtones

question: Why are sine and cosine functions considered the building blocks of harmonic signal theory?

choices.A: Because they are the easiest to generate
choices.B: Because any periodic signal can be represented as a sum of sine and cosine waves
choices.C: Because they are the only functions that exist

question: What are partials, overtones, or harmonics in a complex signal?

choices.B: Multiple frequencies that are added together with different intensities to create complex signals
choices.C: Unwanted noise that distorts the signal

question: What is the mathematical representation of a complex signal with multiple frequencies?

choices.B: s(t) = a₀f₀(t) + a₁f₁(t) + ... + aₙfₙ(t)

question: What defines a harmonic signal?

choices.A: All frequencies are random and unrelated
choices.B: The frequencies have integer ratios between the fundamental (f₀) and the other partials
choices.C: The signal contains only one frequency
choices.D: The signal has no frequency components at all

question: If a harmonic signal has a fundamental frequency of 100 Hz, what would be the frequencies of its next two partials?

choices.B: 200 Hz and 300 Hz

question: What type of sounds are most musical sounds (approximately)?

choices.B: Harmonic

question: What type of sounds do bells typically produce?

choices.B: Inharmonic sounds

question: What is the key characteristic of an inharmonic signal?

choices.A: All partials are at integer multiples of the fundamental
choices.B: The partials are NOT at integer multiples of the fundamental

question: If a sound has frequency components at 100 Hz, 201 Hz, and 305 Hz, how would you classify it?

choices.B: Inharmonic (the ratios are not exact integers)

question: Why do bells produce inharmonic sounds?

choices.A: Because they are made of metal
choices.B: Because their complex shape causes vibrations at non-integer related frequencies
choices.C: Because they are struck with a hammer

question: What happens when a guitar string is plucked?

choices.A: It vibrates only at its fundamental frequency
choices.B: It vibrates in multiple modes simultaneously, creating harmonic partials

question: What are the different modes of vibration on a guitar string?

choices.B: The whole string, halves, thirds, etc. vibrating simultaneously

question: If you lightly touch a guitar string at its midpoint and pluck, what sound will you hear?

choices.B: An octave higher (double the frequency)

question: If you lightly touch a guitar string at the 7th fret position (1/3 of the string length) and pluck, what happens?

choices.B: You hear triple the frequency (a harmonic)

question: What is the relationship between the different vibration modes on a harmonic string?

choices.B: They are integer multiples of each other
choices.C: They are all the same frequency

question: How do harmonic signals sound to our ears compared to inharmonic signals?

choices.B: Harmonic sounds are smoother and more musical

question: What is an example of an everyday sound that is approximately harmonic?

choices.B: A guitar note

question: What is an example of an everyday sound that is typically inharmonic?

choices.B: A bell ringing

question: In the equation s(t) = a₀f₀(t) + a₁f₁(t) + ... + aₙfₙ(t), what do the coefficients a₀, a₁, etc. represent?

choices.B: The intensities or amplitudes of each frequency component

question: If a harmonic signal has a strong fundamental and weaker harmonics, what does this mean for the sound?

choices.A: The fundamental frequency dominates the perceived pitch
choices.B: The harmonics dominate the perceived pitch

question: What gives different musical instruments their unique timbre (tone color) even when playing the same note?

choices.B: The different relative intensities (aₙ coefficients) of their harmonic partials

question: Why does a flute sound different from a violin playing the same note?

choices.B: They have different harmonic content (different strengths of partials)
choices.C: One is louder than the other
choices.D: One is longer than the other

question: What would a signal sound like if it contained only the fundamental frequency with no harmonics?

choices.B: A pure, simple tone (like a tuning fork)

question: How do dial tones (like in old telephones) typically sound?

choices.A: They are harmonic with many overtones
choices.B: They are a mix of two sinusoids (frequencies) that are often inharmonic

question: Why can you clearly hear two different tones in a dial tone?

choices.B: Because they are non-harmonic frequencies that don't blend together
choices.D: Because they are the same frequency

question: What happens when frequencies are integer multiples of each other (harmonic)?

choices.B: They blend together smoothly and are perceived as one sound with a rich tone

question: What happens when frequencies are non-integer multiples of each other (inharmonic)?

choices.B: They are perceived as separate, distinct tones
choices.D: They create a pure sine wave

question: In a harmonic series, if the fundamental is 200 Hz, what is the frequency of the 3rd harmonic?

choices.B: 600 Hz

question: In a harmonic series, if the fundamental is 150 Hz, what is the frequency of the 4th harmonic?

choices.B: 450 Hz

question: What is the relationship between the first mode of vibration and the second mode on a harmonic string?

choices.A: The second mode is half the frequency
choices.B: The second mode is double the frequency

question: On a guitar string, where is the node (point of no vibration) for the second harmonic?

choices.B: At the midpoint of the string

question: What happens to the harmonic content when you change the point where you pluck a guitar string?

choices.B: The relative strengths (amplitudes) of different harmonics change

question: Why does plucking a guitar string near the bridge produce a brighter, more treble sound?

choices.B: Because it emphasizes higher harmonics
choices.D: Because it's closer to the ear

question: Why does plucking a guitar string over the soundhole produce a warmer, more mellow sound?

choices.A: Because it emphasizes the fundamental and lower harmonics
choices.B: Because it's quieter
choices.C: Because it's farther from the bridge
choices.D: Because the string is looser there

question: What is the difference between the sound of a xylophone (harmonic) and a bell (inharmonic)?

choices.A: The xylophone has a clear, definite pitch; the bell has a more complex, less definite pitch
choices.B: The xylophone is louder

question: What gives a bell its characteristic 'clang' sound?

choices.B: Its inharmonic partials that don't align with integer multiples

question: How do the partials of a piano string behave compared to an ideal harmonic string?

choices.B: They are slightly inharmonic due to string stiffness

question: What is inharmonicity in the context of piano strings?

choices.B: Slight stretching of partials to higher frequencies due to string stiffness

question: Why do piano tuners stretch the tuning on high and low ends of the piano?

choices.B: To compensate for the inharmonicity of the strings

question: If a sound has partials at 200 Hz, 300 Hz, and 400 Hz, is it harmonic or inharmonic?

choices.B: Inharmonic (1.5 is not an integer ratio)

question: If a sound has partials at 200 Hz, 400 Hz, and 600 Hz, is it harmonic or inharmonic?

choices.B: Inharmonic

question: What is the term for the lowest frequency in a harmonic series?

choices.B: The fundamental

question: What is another name for the partials above the fundamental in a harmonic series?

choices.B: Overtones or harmonics

question: In a harmonic series, the second harmonic is how many times the fundamental frequency?

choices.B: 2 times

question: In a harmonic series, the third harmonic is how many times the fundamental frequency?

choices.B: 2 times

question: What is the musical interval between the fundamental and the second harmonic?

choices.B: An octave

question: What is the musical interval between the second and third harmonics?

choices.B: A perfect fifth

question: What is the musical interval between the third and fourth harmonics?

choices.B: A perfect fifth

question: Why do harmonic intervals like octaves and fifths sound consonant?

choices.A: Because they are simple frequency ratios (2:1, 3:2)
choices.B: Because they are complex ratios

question: Why do inharmonic combinations often sound dissonant?

choices.B: Because they have complex, non-integer frequency ratios

question: In the simple harmonic motion equation, what does √(k/m) determine?

choices.B: The angular frequency of the system

question: Why does the frequency formula f = (1/2π)√(k/m) include 2π?

choices.A: Because it makes the numbers smaller
choices.B: Because 2π is the period of the sine function (one full cycle in radians)

question: In an LC electrical circuit, what determines the resonant frequency?

choices.B: The current only
choices.C: The capacitance and inductance (analogous to k and m)

question: What is a pure tone in audio terms?

choices.B: A sine wave at a single frequency

question: What is sampling in the context of digital signals?

choices.A: Converting continuous time to discrete time points
choices.B: Rounding continuous amplitudes to discrete levels

question: What is quantization?

choices.A: Converting continuous time to discrete time
choices.B: Converting continuous amplitudes to discrete levels

question: If a signal is sampled at 44.1 kHz, what does that mean?

choices.B: 44,100 bits are used per sample

question: What is the Nyquist frequency?

choices.B: Twice the sampling frequency

question: What happens if a signal contains frequencies above the Nyquist frequency?

choices.B: They cause aliasing, appearing as lower frequencies

question: How many bits are needed to represent 256 different amplitude levels?

choices.B: 16 bits

question: What is quantization error?

choices.A: The difference between the original analog signal and the sampled digital value
choices.B: The loss of high frequencies
choices.C: The noise introduced by the ADC
choices.D: The rounding error when converting continuous amplitude to discrete levels

question: If you increase the bit depth, what happens to quantization error?

choices.B: It decreases

question: What is the main advantage of higher sampling rates?

choices.B: Ability to represent higher frequencies

question: What is aliasing?

choices.A: The process of converting analog to digital
choices.B: A phenomenon where high frequencies masquerade as lower frequencies due to insufficient sampling

question: In images, what is spatial aliasing also known as?

choices.B: Pixelation

question: How can aliasing be prevented before sampling?

choices.A: By using a low-pass anti-aliasing filter to remove frequencies above Nyquist
choices.B: By increasing the bit depth
choices.D: By sampling at a lower rate

question: What is the purpose of a reconstruction filter after DAC?

choices.B: To smooth the stepped output and remove high-frequency images

question: What type of waveform does a simple oscillator produce?

choices.B: Periodic waveforms like sine, square, sawtooth

question: What is white noise?

choices.A: Noise with equal energy at all frequencies
choices.B: Noise with more energy at low frequencies

question: What does an envelope generator like ADSR control?

choices.B: The amplitude (volume) over time

question: In an ADSR envelope, what does 'S' stand for?

choices.B: Start time

question: What does the dot product of two signals measure?

choices.B: Their similarity (raw correlation)

question: How is the dot product computed?

choices.A: By multiplying corresponding samples and summing
choices.B: By subtracting one signal from another
choices.C: By taking the average of both signals

question: What is autocorrelation?

choices.B: Correlation of a signal with itself at different lags
choices.D: The Fourier transform of the signal

question: Autocorrelation at lag 0 gives:

choices.B: The energy of the signal

question: What is cross-correlation used for?

choices.A: Measuring similarity between two signals at different time lags
choices.B: Finding the frequency spectrum

question: If you add two identical signals, the energy of the sum is:

choices.B: Four times the energy of one

question: If you add two signals that are exact opposites, the energy of the sum is:

choices.B: Four times

question: What is the correlation coefficient ρ?

choices.B: A normalized measure of correlation, independent of scaling

question: If two signals are perfectly correlated (ρ = 1), what does that imply?

choices.A: They are identical up to a positive scaling factor
choices.B: They are exactly the same

question: What does it mean if two signals have a correlation coefficient of 0?

choices.B: They are uncorrelated

question: In the context of mono compatibility, a correlation coefficient close to 1 indicates:

choices.B: Good mono compatibility

question: What is the Fourier transform used for?

choices.A: To convert a signal from time domain to frequency domain
choices.B: To increase the sampling rate

question: The Fourier transform decomposes a signal into:

choices.A: A sum of sine and cosine waves of different frequencies
choices.B: A series of impulses

question: What is the spectrum of a signal?

choices.A: The amplitude of the signal per frequency
choices.B: The time-domain waveform

question: In the discrete Fourier transform (DFT), what does the index k represent?

choices.B: Frequency bin (k cycles over the signal length)

question: What is the fundamental frequency in a DFT of a signal of length N?

choices.B: N cycles per sample

question: Why are both sine and cosine needed in Fourier analysis?

choices.A: To capture both amplitude and phase of each frequency
choices.B: To double the resolution

question: What is the main advantage of the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) over the DFT?

choices.B: It is computationally faster (O(N log N) vs O(N²))
choices.C: It works on any signal length

question: Parseval's theorem states that:

choices.A: The energy in the time domain equals the energy in the frequency domain (up to a factor)
choices.B: The Fourier transform is linear
choices.C: Convolution in time equals multiplication in frequency

question: For a real-valued signal, the magnitude spectrum is:

choices.B: Even symmetric

question: What is the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)?

choices.A: A Fourier transform applied to the entire signal at once
choices.B: A series of FFTs applied to overlapping windowed segments of a signal
choices.C: A faster version of the FFT
choices.D: A transform that only gives magnitude

question: What is a spectrogram?

choices.B: A 2D image showing frequency content over time, with intensity representing magnitude
choices.C: A graph of amplitude over time

question: Why is windowing used in STFT?

choices.B: To reduce spectral leakage caused by non-periodic segments

question: What is spectral leakage?

choices.B: Spreading of energy from one frequency bin to neighboring bins due to windowing

question: Which window function smoothly tapers to zero at the edges to reduce leakage?

choices.B: Hann window
choices.D: All of the above, but Hann is common

question: In the time domain representation of an analog signal, what is the mathematical notation used to describe it as a function?

choices.B: xₐ(t): ℝ → ℝ

question: What does the notation xₐ(t): ℝ → ℝ signify about an analog signal?

choices.A: It takes discrete time values and produces discrete amplitudes
choices.B: It takes continuous time values (real numbers) and produces continuous amplitude values (real numbers)
choices.C: It takes two-dimensional spatial coordinates and produces a single value
choices.D: It takes frequency as input and produces complex numbers as output

question: What is the correct mathematical notation for a digital signal in the time domain?

choices.B: x(f): ℝ → ℂ

question: What does the square bracket notation x[n] versus parentheses x(t) indicate in signal processing?

choices.A: x[n] indicates a continuous signal, x(t) indicates a discrete signal
choices.B: x[n] indicates a discrete-time signal, x(t) indicates a continuous-time signal
choices.C: x[n] is for audio signals only, x(t) is for image signals only
choices.D: There is no difference; they are interchangeable

question: What does it mean when the slides state that a digital signal x[n] is approximately equal to xₐ(n/Fs)?

choices.A: The digital signal is an exact copy of the analog signal
choices.B: The digital signal represents the analog signal's amplitude at discrete time points spaced 1/Fs seconds apart
choices.C: The digital signal has Fs times more samples than the analog signal
choices.D: The digital signal is the derivative of the analog signal

question: In the time domain, what operation does y[n] = a·x[n] perform on a signal?

choices.B: Changing the amplitude (scaling) of the signal

question: If |a| > 1 in the operation y[n] = a·x[n] for an audio signal, what is the perceptual result?

choices.B: The sound becomes louder/brighter

question: What mathematical operation represents mixing two signals together in the time domain?

choices.B: y[n] = x₁[n] - x₂[n]

question: What does the time-shifting operation y[n] = x[n - k] accomplish?

choices.A: It changes the amplitude of the signal
choices.B: It delays the signal by k samples
choices.C: It advances the signal by k samples

question: If y[n] = x[n - 5], how many samples is the signal delayed?

choices.B: Delayed by 5 samples
choices.D: No delay, it's the same signal

question: What is the mathematical notation for a 2D analog image signal?

choices.B: xₐ(x,y): ℝ² → ℝ

question: What does the notation xₐ(x,y): ℝ² → ℝ signify for an analog image?

choices.A: It takes one spatial coordinate and time and produces an amplitude
choices.B: It takes two continuous spatial coordinates and produces a continuous intensity/brightness value
choices.C: It takes discrete pixel coordinates and produces discrete brightness values
choices.D: It takes frequency coordinates and produces complex numbers

question: What is the correct mathematical notation for a digital image (grayscale)?

choices.B: x(t): ℝ → ℝ

question: In the digital image notation x[n,m] ≈ xₐ(n/Fs, m/Fs), what do n and m represent?

choices.A: The brightness values at those coordinates
choices.B: The discrete sample indices in the horizontal and vertical directions
choices.C: The sampling frequency in each dimension
choices.D: The continuous spatial coordinates in millimeters

question: In digital imaging, what is the equivalent of sampling frequency in the spatial domain?

choices.A: Bit depth (number of bits per pixel)
choices.B: Colour depth
choices.C: Spatial resolution, often measured in dots per inch (DPI) or pixels per inch (PPI)

question: What does DPI stand for in the context of digital images?

choices.B: Dots Per Inch (a measure of spatial sampling frequency)

question: If an image has a resolution of 300 DPI, what does this mean?

choices.A: There are 300 pixels in the entire image
choices.B: There are 300 samples (pixels) per inch in both dimensions
choices.C: Each pixel uses 300 bits of data

question: For digital images, what determines the spatial sampling frequency?

choices.A: The colour depth of the image
choices.B: The number of pixels per unit distance (e.g., DPI)
choices.D: The bit depth of each pixel

question: In the context of images, what is the equivalent of quantization (from audio)?

choices.B: Image dimensions (width × height)
choices.C: Bit depth (number of bits per pixel, determining the number of possible colours or intensity levels)

question: If a grayscale image uses 8 bits per pixel, how many different intensity levels can each pixel represent?
A colour image uses 24 bits per pixel (8 bits each for Red, Green, and Blue). Approximately how many different colours can it represent?
What visual artifact is equivalent to quantization error in images?

choices.B: Posterisation (banding) - where smooth gradients appear as distinct steps

question: What is the visual equivalent of aliasing in digital images called?

choices.B: Posterisation

question: The slides mention that woven fabric patterns can exceed camera resolution. What is the resulting visual artifact?

choices.B: The colours of the fabric change
choices.C: Moiré patterns appear due to spatial aliasing

question: Why can the aliasing effect appear differently per colour channel in a digital camera?

choices.A: Because different colours have different wavelengths
choices.B: Because cameras use different sampling rates for each colour channel due to the Bayer filter pattern
choices.C: Because red light travels faster than blue light
choices.D: Because JPEG compression affects colours differently

question: In image processing, what is the spatial equivalent of an anti-aliasing filter?

choices.B: An optical low-pass filter (or blurring filter) placed in front of the sensor to remove fine details that would cause aliasing

question: Many digital cameras have an optical low-pass filter (OLPF) or anti-aliasing filter. What is its purpose?

choices.B: To slightly blur the image to prevent moiré patterns caused by spatial aliasing

question: The slides show two sine waves: one low frequency and one high frequency. What is the key visual difference in how they are represented when sampled?

choices.A: The low frequency wave appears jagged, the high frequency appears smooth
choices.B: The high frequency wave may be represented by too few points, making it look like a lower frequency wave
choices.C: The low frequency wave has higher amplitude
choices.D: The high frequency wave has more samples per cycle

question: What is the intuitive rule of thumb for the minimum samples needed per wave cycle to avoid aliasing?

choices.A: One sample per cycle (for the peak only)
choices.B: Two samples per cycle (one for the peak and one for the trough)
choices.C: Ten samples per cycle for accurate representation
choices.D: The number of samples depends on the wave's amplitude

question: According to the slides, what happens to frequencies in a signal that are above the Nyquist frequency?

choices.A: They are stored as high frequencies but marked as aliased
choices.B: They are removed from the signal
choices.C: They get 'mirrored down' at the Nyquist frequency and appear as lower frequencies

question: The slides state that aliasing can occur when sampling mathematical functions to generate digital signals. What is an example of this?

choices.A: Generating a sine wave in code with a frequency above the Nyquist limit for the chosen sample rate
choices.B: Adding two sine waves together
choices.C: Applying a gain to a signal
choices.D: Reading an audio file from disk

question: What are the four main types of filters mentioned in the slides, named by the frequency bands they pass?

choices.B: Shelf, peak, notch, and comb

question: What are common real-world examples of filters mentioned in the slides?

choices.B: Equalizers (EQs) in stereos and blurring/sharpening filters for images

question: According to the slides, what are the two solutions to the aliasing problem?

choices.A: Increase bit depth or use a high-pass filter
choices.B: Increase time/space resolution or use an anti-aliasing filter
choices.C: Use a reconstruction filter or increase the signal amplitude

question: Why might increasing the time or space resolution not always be a practical solution to aliasing?

choices.B: It may not be possible (hardware limits) or practical (file size/storage limits)

question: The slides mention that the output created from a DAC contains additional frequencies. What is the purpose of a reconstruction filter?

choices.A: To add these frequencies back in
choices.B: To convert the signal back to digital
choices.C: To remove these additional high-frequency 'image' components, smoothing the output

question: What is the name of the function that describes the effect of a zero-order hold on the signal's spectrum?

choices.B: A Gaussian function

question: What are the two main categories of noise oscillators mentioned in terms of their frequency distribution?

choices.B: White noise (evenly distributed) and pink noise (weaker high frequencies, 1/f)

question: What does VCF stand for in the context of analog synthesizers?

choices.B: Voltage Controlled Filter

question: What does VCA stand for in the context of analog synthesizers?

choices.B: Variable Capacitance Amplifier

question: In a time-varying gain control unit, y[t] = x[t] * c[t], if c[t] is not constant, what is this unit called?

choices.B: Time-variant gain

question: What is an LFO (Low-Frequency Oscillator) typically used for in a synthesizer?

choices.A: To generate the main audible pitch of a note
choices.B: To modulate parameters like pitch (vibrato) or amplitude (tremolo) at a low rate

question: What does ADSR stand for in an envelope generator?

choices.B: Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release

question: In an ADSR envelope, which stage determines how long it takes for the sound to reach its maximum level after a note is triggered?

choices.B: Sustain

question: In an ADSR envelope, which stage determines the level that the sound holds as long as the key is pressed (after the initial Attack and Decay)?

choices.B: Decay

question: In an ADSR envelope, which stage determines how long it takes for the sound to fade to zero after the key is released?

choices.B: Decay

question: According to the slides, which parameters of an ADSR envelope are typically 'rate' or 'time' parameters, and which is a 'level' parameter?

choices.B: All are level parameters
choices.C: Attack, Decay, and Release are rates; Sustain is a level
choices.D: Attack and Release are rates; Decay and Sustain are levels

question: In a digital system, what does a gain control unit do to every sample?

choices.B: It multiplies the sample with a gain factor
choices.C: It adds noise to the sample

question: What is the purpose of MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) as mentioned in the slides?

choices.B: To allow external controllers, like a keyboard, to communicate with synthesizers
choices.D: To convert analog to digital signals

question: In a typical ADSR envelope, what event typically triggers the Attack, Decay, and Sustain stages?

choices.B: A MIDI note-on message

question: What event typically triggers the Release stage of an ADSR envelope?

choices.B: The LFO reaching its peak
choices.D: The end of the Decay stage

question: What is the trade-off when choosing window length in STFT?

choices.A: Longer windows give better time resolution but worse frequency resolution
choices.B: Longer windows give better frequency resolution but worse time resolution
choices.C: Shorter windows give both better resolutions
choices.D: Window length does not affect resolution

question: What is resynthesis in the context of STFT?

choices.A: Reconstructing the time-domain signal from modified spectra
choices.B: Taking the FFT again

question: How can equalization be performed using the STFT?

choices.A: By multiplying each frequency bin in the spectrum by a gain factor
choices.B: By adding a constant to all samples

question: What is convolution?

choices.A: A mathematical operation that combines two signals to produce a third
choices.B: The same as correlation
choices.D: The inverse of the Fourier transform

question: The convolution theorem states that:

choices.A: Convolution in time domain equals multiplication in frequency domain
choices.B: Multiplication in time equals convolution in frequency
choices.D: Convolution is the same as correlation

question: What is the impulse response of a system?

choices.A: The output when the input is a unit impulse
choices.B: The frequency response

question: In an FIR filter, the impulse response is:

choices.B: Exactly the filter coefficients
choices.D: The same as the frequency response

question: What is a linear filter?

choices.A: A filter that changes the signal nonlinearly
choices.B: A filter that satisfies superposition: f(ax+by) = a f(x) + b f(y)

question: What is the difference between FIR and IIR filters?

choices.A: FIR uses only input, IIR uses input and output feedback
choices.B: FIR is faster, IIR is slower
choices.C: FIR has infinite impulse response, IIR has finite
choices.D: FIR is analog, IIR is digital

question: What does the order of a filter refer to?

choices.A: The number of filter coefficients minus one
choices.B: The cutoff frequency

question: What is a low-pass filter?

choices.A: It allows high frequencies to pass and attenuates low frequencies
choices.B: It allows low frequencies to pass and attenuates high frequencies
choices.C: It allows a band of frequencies to pass
choices.D: It stops a band of frequencies

question: What is a high-pass filter used for?

choices.B: Removing high-frequency noise

question: What is a band-pass filter?

choices.A: It passes frequencies above a cutoff
choices.B: It passes frequencies within a certain range and attenuates others

question: What is a band-stop (notch) filter?

choices.A: It attenuates a specific range of frequencies
choices.B: It passes only low frequencies

question: What is a comb filter?

choices.A: A filter with a frequency response that looks like a comb, caused by short delays
choices.B: A filter that removes all frequencies
choices.C: A filter that only lets through one frequency
choices.D: A filter with a flat response

question: What is an all-pass filter?

choices.A: It passes all frequencies with equal magnitude but modifies phase
choices.B: It blocks all frequencies
choices.C: It only passes a single frequency

question: What is a shelving filter?

choices.A: It attenuates or boosts frequencies above or below a cutoff by a fixed amount
choices.B: It has a steep roll-off
choices.D: It is used only in graphics

question: What does the cutoff frequency of a filter mean?

choices.A: The frequency at which the filter's response is -3 dB relative to the passband
choices.B: The highest frequency that can pass
choices.C: The lowest frequency that can pass

question: What is filter slope measured in?

choices.B: Hz

question: What is ripple in a filter's frequency response?

choices.A: Variation in the passband or stopband
choices.B: A peak at the cutoff

question: What is resonance in a filter?

choices.A: A peak in the frequency response near the cutoff
choices.B: A dip in the response
choices.C: The ability to pass all frequencies

question: Why are IIR filters more efficient than FIR filters for a given cutoff sharpness?

choices.A: They use feedback, achieving steeper roll-off with fewer coefficients
choices.B: They are simpler to design

question: What is a potential drawback of IIR filters?

choices.A: They can become unstable due to feedback
choices.B: They require more computation

question: In Python, which function is used to design an FIR filter?

choices.B: scipy.signal.iirdesign

question: What does the 'sos' output format for IIR filters provide?

choices.A: Second-order sections for better numerical stability
choices.B: Single coefficient arrays

question: In games, why are real-time effects often implemented in the time domain?

choices.A: Because frequency domain processing is too slow for real-time
choices.B: Because time domain is more accurate

question: What is subtractive synthesis?

choices.A: Generating a sound by adding sine waves
choices.B: Starting with a harmonically rich waveform and filtering out frequencies

question: In a typical subtractive synthesizer, what does the VCF (Voltage Controlled Filter) do?

choices.B: Shapes the frequency content of the sound

question: What is an LFO (Low-Frequency Oscillator) used for in synthesis?

choices.B: To modulate parameters like filter cutoff or amplitude over time

question: What is convolution reverb?

choices.A: A reverb that uses a recorded impulse response of a real space
choices.B: A simple delay-based reverb

question: How is a flanger effect created?

choices.A: By using a time-varying short delay, creating a comb filter that sweeps
choices.B: By adding a constant delay

question: What does an ADSR envelope control in a synthesizer?

choices.B: The volume over time (amplitude envelope)

question: How can DSP be used in data analysis for financial time series?

choices.A: By applying filters to smooth out noise and identify trends
choices.B: By converting prices to images

question: What is a common feature used in machine learning for audio analysis?

choices.B: Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs)

question: Why are spectrograms often used as input to CNNs for audio classification?

choices.A: They represent time-frequency information in a 2D image-like format
choices.B: They are smaller than waveforms

question: In image processing, what does a low-pass filter do?

choices.B: Blurs the image by removing high-frequency details

question: What is the purpose of a high-pass filter in image processing?

choices.B: To enhance edges and fine details

question: How can cross-correlation be used in data analysis?

choices.A: To find time delays between two related signals
choices.B: To compute the Fourier transform

question: How do tones and noise differ in terms of frequency content?

choices.A: Tones contain energy at discrete frequency points, while noise contains energy at all frequencies
choices.B: Tones are louder than noise
choices.C: Tones are always harmonic, noise is always inharmonic
choices.D: Tones exist only in digital signals, noise only in analog

question: What is white noise?

choices.A: Noise with energy concentrated at low frequencies
choices.B: Noise with evenly distributed energy at all frequencies
choices.C: Noise with no energy at any frequency
choices.D: A pure tone at a single frequency

question: What is the analog radio equivalent of noise when not tuned to a station?

choices.B: Silence

question: What causes the grainy appearance in photos taken in low light?

choices.B: Noise in the image signal

question: Why does noise in images appear more noticeable in low-light photography?

choices.A: Because the camera is less stable
choices.B: Because the signal (light) is weak, making the noise more prominent relative to the signal
choices.C: Because low light causes more noise
choices.D: Because the shutter speed is slower

question: In the mathematical representation of a digital signal, x[n] ≈ x_a(n/Fs), what does the variable 'n' represent?

choices.A: The amplitude of the signal at a specific time
choices.B: The discrete-time sample index (an integer)

question: In the relation x[n] ≈ x_a(n/Fs), what does 'Fs' stand for and what are its units?

choices.A: Sampling Frequency, measured in Hertz (samples per second)
choices.B: Signal Frequency, measured in Hertz

question: If a signal is sampled at a rate of 48 kHz, how much time elapses between two consecutive samples, n and n+1?

choices.B: 1/48,000 seconds (approximately 20.83 microseconds)

question: What are the two fundamental processes involved in converting an analog signal to a digital signal?

choices.B: Sampling (time discretisation) and Quantisation (amplitude discretisation)

question: In the context of digitisation, what does 'sample/hold' refer to?

choices.A: A process of storing samples in memory
choices.B: An electronic circuit that takes a value at regular time intervals and holds it constant until the next sample
choices.C: A technique for increasing the sample rate
choices.D: A method for quantising a signal

question: What is the spatial equivalent of sampling frequency in a digital image?

choices.B: Colour saturation
choices.C: Raster size or dots per inch (DPI)

question: What is the primary effect of increasing the sampling rate of a digital signal?

choices.A: It increases the amplitude (loudness) of the signal.
choices.B: It increases the maximum frequency that can be accurately represented.
choices.C: It decreases the file size of the digital signal.
choices.D: It reduces the number of bits needed per sample.

question: A 16-bit system has a sample resolution of 16 bits. Approximately how many different discrete amplitude levels can it represent?
An 8-bit system can represent 256 discrete levels. How many levels can a 24-bit system represent?
What is 'quantisation error'?

choices.A: The loss of high-frequency information during sampling
choices.B: The rounding error that occurs when mapping a continuous amplitude value to the nearest discrete level
choices.C: The time delay introduced by the ADC conversion process
choices.D: The noise generated by the analogue electronics

question: How does increasing the bit depth (sample resolution) affect quantisation error and signal quality?

choices.A: It increases quantisation error, leading to lower signal quality.
choices.B: It decreases quantisation error, leading to a more accurate representation of the original signal.
choices.C: It has no effect on quantisation error, only on file size.

question: Which of the following combinations will result in the highest quality digital audio, assuming all other factors are equal?

choices.B: 44.1 kHz sampling rate, 16-bit depth

question: According to the intuitive explanation in the slides, what is the minimum number of samples required per cycle of a waveform to represent its frequency correctly?

choices.B: Two samples per cycle
choices.D: It depends on the amplitude of the wave

question: What is the phenomenon called where a high-frequency signal, when sampled, takes on the identity of a lower-frequency signal?

choices.B: Filtering

question: In the context of digital images, what is a common visual manifestation of aliasing known as?

choices.B: Moiré pattern

question: According to the Sampling Theorem, what is the Nyquist frequency?

choices.A: The maximum frequency that can be accurately captured, which is equal to the sampling frequency (Fs).
choices.B: The minimum frequency that can be captured, which is half the sampling frequency (Fs/2).
choices.C: The maximum frequency that can be accurately captured, which is half the sampling frequency (Fs/2).
choices.D: The frequency at which aliasing begins, which is equal to the sampling frequency (Fs).

question: If a signal is sampled at 40 kHz, what is the highest frequency that can be accurately represented without aliasing?

choices.B: 20 kHz

question: An audio signal contains a strong frequency component at 25 kHz. It is sampled at a rate of 44.1 kHz. What will happen to this 25 kHz component?

choices.A: It will be accurately recorded as 25 kHz.
choices.B: It will be filtered out automatically.
choices.C: It will be aliased and appear as a lower frequency, specifically |25 - 44.1| = 19.1 kHz.
choices.D: It will be quantised into noise.

question: In the example above, the 25 kHz component is above the Nyquist frequency (22.05 kHz). To what frequency will it be aliased, based on the 'mirroring' concept?

choices.B: 44.1 kHz - 25 kHz = 19.1 kHz

question: The slides state that aliasing can occur not only when sampling physical signals, but also within the digital domain. Which process is given as an example of this?

choices.B: Down-sampling (reducing the sample rate of) a digital signal

question: What is the primary purpose of an anti-aliasing filter?

choices.A: To boost high frequencies before sampling
choices.B: To remove quantization noise after sampling
choices.C: To remove frequency components above the Nyquist frequency *before* the signal is sampled
choices.D: To reconstruct the original signal from its samples

question: What type of filter is typically used as an anti-aliasing filter?

choices.B: A band-pass filter
choices.C: A low-pass filter with a cutoff at or below the Nyquist frequency

question: In a typical Analog-to-Digital (ADC) chain, where is the anti-aliasing filter placed?

choices.A: After the ADC, before the digital signal is stored.
choices.B: Before the ADC, operating on the continuous analog signal.
choices.D: It is not used in a standard ADC chain.

question: What is the purpose of a reconstruction filter, which is placed after a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)?

choices.A: To remove low-frequency noise from the output.
choices.B: To smooth the 'stepped' output of the DAC by removing high-frequency image spectra created during the conversion process.
choices.D: To convert the signal back to digital.

question: The slides mention that an 'ideal impulses' reconstruction is not practical. What is a more practical, but still imperfect, method of reconstruction mentioned?

choices.B: Zero-order hold (holding the value of the sample)

question: In the context of the complete Analog-Digital-Analog chain, what are the two stages where low-pass filtering is essential?

choices.B: Encoding and Decoding
choices.C: Anti-aliasing filtering (before ADC) and Reconstruction filtering (after DAC)

question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a typical simple periodic waveform generated by an oscillator?

choices.B: Square wave

question: The slides mention different 'colours' of noise. What is the defining characteristic of pink noise?

choices.A: It has evenly distributed energy at all frequencies (white noise).
choices.B: It has weaker high-frequency components, with amplitude proportional to 1/f.
choices.C: It has energy only at a single frequency.
choices.D: It has stronger high-frequency components, with amplitude proportional to f.

question: If you double the amplitude of a digital signal sample by sample, what basic operation are you performing?

choices.B: Delaying

question: In the equation for a time-varying gain control unit, y[n] = x[n] * c[n], what does c[n] represent?

choices.B: The original signal value
choices.C: A gain factor that can change for each sample n

question: What is the role of an envelope generator in a synthesiser?

choices.A: To create the initial pitched sound.
choices.B: To shape how a sound's properties (like amplitude or filter cutoff) change over time.
choices.C: To add reverberation to the sound.

question: In an ADSR envelope generator, what do the four stages represent?

choices.B: Attack, Decay, Sustain, and Release

question: In an ADSR envelope, which stage's parameter is typically a 'level' (target amplitude), while the others are 'rate' or 'time' parameters?

choices.B: Decay

question: Define an analog signal in mathematical terms, as shown in the slides.

choices.B: xₐ(t): ℝ → ℝ (a function mapping continuous time to continuous amplitude)

question: Define a digital signal in mathematical terms, as shown in the slides.

choices.B: xₐ(x,y): ℝ² → ℝ
choices.C: x[n]: ℤ → ℝ or ℤ (a function mapping discrete time indices to discrete amplitude values)

question: Define a 2D analog image signal in mathematical terms.

choices.B: xₐ(x,y): ℝ² → ℝ (a function mapping two continuous spatial coordinates to a continuous intensity value)

question: Define a 2D digital image signal in mathematical terms.

choices.B: xₐ(x,y): ℝ² → ℝ
choices.C: x[n,m]: ℤ² → ℤ (a function mapping two discrete spatial indices to a discrete intensity value)

question: Define Sampling Frequency (Fs).

choices.A: The number of bits used to represent each sample
choices.B: The number of samples taken per unit of time (e.g., per second), measured in Hertz
choices.C: The highest frequency that can be represented in a digital signal
choices.D: The time between two consecutive samples

question: What is the relationship between a digital signal x[n] and its analog counterpart xₐ(t) using the sampling frequency?

choices.B: x[n] ≈ xₐ(n / Fs)

question: Define the amplitude scaling operation in the time domain.

choices.B: y[n] = x₁[n] + x₂[n]
choices.C: y[n] = a · x[n], where 'a' is a scaling factor

question: Define the signal mixing (addition) operation in the time domain.

choices.B: y[n] = x[n - k]

question: Define the delay (time-shifting) operation in the time domain.

choices.B: y[n] = x₁[n] + x₂[n]

question: Define the spectrum of a signal.

choices.A: The representation of a signal's amplitude over time
choices.B: The amplitude (and often phase) of the signal per frequency component
choices.C: The total energy contained in the signal
choices.D: The sampling rate of the signal

question: Define the power spectrum of a signal.

choices.A: The amplitude of each frequency component
choices.B: The phase of each frequency component
choices.C: The pointwise square of the spectrum (amplitude squared per frequency)
choices.D: The Fourier transform of the signal

question: Define sampling in the context of digitisation.

choices.A: The process of rounding continuous amplitude values to discrete levels
choices.B: The process of converting a continuous-time signal into a discrete-time signal by taking values at regular intervals
choices.C: The process of filtering out high frequencies
choices.D: The process of amplifying a signal

question: Define quantization in the context of digitisation.

choices.A: The process of taking samples at regular time intervals
choices.B: The process of mapping a continuous range of amplitudes to a discrete set of amplitude levels
choices.C: The process of converting digital back to analog
choices.D: The process of compressing a digital signal

question: Define sample resolution (bit depth).

choices.A: The number of samples taken per second
choices.B: The number of bits used to represent each sample, defining the number of possible discrete amplitude levels
choices.C: The physical size of each sample in memory
choices.D: The frequency range that can be represented

question: Define quantization error.

choices.A: The loss of high frequencies during sampling
choices.B: The difference between the original continuous amplitude value and its quantized discrete value (the rounding error)
choices.C: The time delay introduced by the ADC
choices.D: The noise floor of the recording system

question: Define the Nyquist frequency.

choices.B: Half the sampling frequency (Fs/2), which is the maximum frequency that can be accurately represented without aliasing
choices.D: The frequency at which quantization error is minimized

question: Define aliasing.

choices.A: The process of converting analog to digital
choices.B: A phenomenon where frequency components above the Nyquist frequency are misrepresented as lower frequencies in the sampled signal
choices.D: The amplification of certain frequencies by a filter

question: State the Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem) in simple terms.

choices.A: To accurately represent a signal, the sampling frequency must be less than the highest frequency in the signal.
choices.B: To accurately represent a signal, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency present in the signal.
choices.C: The bit depth must be at least 16 bits for good quality.
choices.D: Quantization error is inversely proportional to the sampling rate.

question: Define a filter in the context of signal processing.

choices.A: A device or process that stores digital signals
choices.B: A signal processing unit designed to remove or attenuate certain frequency components from a signal
choices.C: A type of oscillator that generates waveforms

question: Define a low-pass filter.

choices.A: A filter that allows high frequencies to pass and attenuates low frequencies
choices.B: A filter that allows frequencies below a cutoff frequency to pass and attenuates frequencies above it
choices.C: A filter that allows a specific band of frequencies to pass
choices.D: A filter that attenuates a specific band of frequencies

question: Define a high-pass filter.

choices.A: A filter that allows frequencies above a cutoff frequency to pass and attenuates frequencies below it
choices.B: A filter that allows low frequencies to pass
choices.C: A filter that passes all frequencies equally
choices.D: A filter that removes high frequencies

question: Define a band-pass filter.

choices.A: A filter that passes frequencies below a cutoff
choices.B: A filter that passes frequencies above a cutoff
choices.C: A filter that passes frequencies within a certain range and attenuates frequencies outside that range
choices.D: A filter that attenuates a specific range of frequencies

question: Define a band-reject (notch) filter.

choices.A: A filter that passes all frequencies except those within a specific stopband
choices.B: A filter that passes only low frequencies
choices.C: A filter that passes only high frequencies
choices.D: A filter that boosts all frequencies

question: Define an anti-aliasing filter.

choices.A: A filter used after DAC to smooth the output
choices.B: A low-pass filter applied to an analog signal before sampling to remove frequency components above the Nyquist frequency
choices.C: A filter that enhances high frequencies
choices.D: A filter that reduces quantization noise

question: Define a reconstruction filter.

choices.A: A low-pass filter applied to the output of a DAC to remove high-frequency image spectra and smooth the waveform
choices.B: A filter applied before sampling
choices.C: A filter that boosts the signal's amplitude
choices.D: A filter that converts analog to digital

question: Define the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) reconstruction method.

choices.B: Holding each sample value constant until the next sample, creating a 'staircase' waveform
choices.C: Generating ideal impulses at each sample point
choices.D: Using a mathematical function to perfectly reconstruct the signal

question: Define a sinc function (sin(x)/x) in the context of reconstruction.

choices.A: A function used to generate square waves
choices.B: The shape of the ideal reconstruction filter in the time domain, and the function that describes the effect of zero-order hold on the spectrum
choices.D: The mathematical representation of white noise

question: Define an oscillator in the context of signal generation.

choices.B: A simple signal generator that produces periodic waveforms (e.g., sine, square, sawtooth)

question: Define a periodic waveform.

choices.A: A waveform that repeats itself at regular intervals (with a fixed period)
choices.B: A random, non-repeating waveform
choices.C: A waveform that constantly increases in frequency

question: Define harmonic signals (in the context of periodic waveforms).

choices.A: Signals that contain only one frequency
choices.B: Signals that contain frequency components at integer multiples of a fundamental frequency

question: Define white noise.

choices.A: Noise with energy concentrated at low frequencies
choices.B: Noise with energy concentrated at high frequencies
choices.C: Noise that has equal energy per frequency (flat spectrum)
choices.D: A pure tone at a single frequency

question: Define pink noise.

choices.A: Noise with equal energy at all frequencies
choices.B: Noise whose amplitude decreases with increasing frequency, proportional to 1/f (equal energy per octave)
choices.C: Noise whose amplitude increases with frequency

question: Define a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO).

choices.A: An oscillator whose frequency is controlled by an external voltage
choices.B: An oscillator that generates a constant frequency

question: Define a Voltage Controlled Filter (VCF).

choices.B: A filter whose cutoff frequency or resonance is controlled by an external voltage

question: Define a Voltage Controlled Amplifier (VCA).

choices.A: An oscillator whose amplitude is controlled by voltage
choices.B: A filter whose gain is controlled by voltage
choices.C: An amplifier whose gain (amplitude) is controlled by an external voltage

question: Define MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface).

choices.A: A protocol for storing high-quality digital audio
choices.B: A technical standard that describes a protocol, digital interface, and connectors for communicating musical performance data (like note-on, note-off, control changes)

question: Define an LFO (Low-Frequency Oscillator).

choices.A: An oscillator that generates audio-rate signals (20 Hz - 20 kHz)
choices.B: An oscillator that generates very low frequencies (typically below 20 Hz) used for modulation purposes (e.g., vibrato, tremolo)
choices.C: A filter that removes low frequencies

question: Define an Envelope Generator (specifically ADSR).

choices.A: A device that generates periodic waveforms
choices.B: A modulator that shapes a parameter (like amplitude or filter cutoff) over time in response to an event (like a key press)
choices.D: A filter that removes high frequencies

question: Define the 'Attack' stage of an ADSR envelope.

choices.A: The time it takes for the sound to fade to zero after the key is released
choices.B: The time it takes for the sound to rise from zero to its maximum level after the key is pressed
choices.C: The time it takes for the sound to fall from the attack level to the sustain level
choices.D: The level that the sound holds while the key is pressed

question: Define the 'Decay' stage of an ADSR envelope.

choices.A: The initial rise time of the sound
choices.B: The time it takes for the sound to fall from the attack peak to the designated sustain level
choices.C: The level maintained while the key is held
choices.D: The fade-out time after the key is released

question: Define the 'Sustain' stage of an ADSR envelope.

choices.A: The time it takes for the sound to reach its peak
choices.B: The time it takes to drop to the holding level
choices.C: The level (amplitude) that the sound maintains for as long as the key is held down
choices.D: The time it takes for the sound to fade out

question: Define the 'Release' stage of an ADSR envelope.

choices.A: The initial rise of the sound
choices.B: The drop from peak to sustain level
choices.D: The time it takes for the sound to decay from the sustain level to zero after the key is released

question: Define the period (p) of a periodic signal.

choices.A: The number of cycles per second
choices.B: The duration of one complete cycle of the signal
choices.D: The phase of the signal at time zero

question: Define frequency (f) of a periodic signal.

choices.A: The duration of one complete cycle
choices.B: The number of cycles per unit time (f = 1/p), measured in Hertz
choices.D: The time delay of the signal

question: Define amplitude in the context of a wave or signal.

choices.A: The number of cycles per second
choices.B: The time for one complete cycle
choices.C: The strength or magnitude of the oscillation (e.g., loudness for sound, brightness for light)

question: Define decibel (dB) as a measurement unit.

choices.B: A logarithmic unit that expresses the ratio of two quantities (e.g., power or amplitude)

question: Define signal energy in the context of discrete-time signals.

choices.A: The sum of the absolute values of the signal
choices.B: The sum of the squares of the signal values (Σ |x[n]|²)
choices.C: The maximum value of the signal
choices.D: The average value of the signal

question: Define signal power.

choices.B: The energy of the signal divided by time (average energy per unit time)

question: Define inharmonic signal.

choices.A: A signal whose frequency components are at integer multiples of a fundamental
choices.B: A signal whose frequency components are NOT at integer multiples of a fundamental
choices.C: A signal with only one frequency component
choices.D: A signal with no frequency components

question: Define timbre (tone color).

choices.A: The fundamental frequency of a sound
choices.B: The loudness of a sound
choices.C: The characteristic quality of a sound that distinguishes different instruments playing the same note, determined by the relative strengths of its harmonic partials
